text: My flower garden. My name is Anne. I love flowers. I have a flower garden. My garden is in front of my house.  My neighbor has a garden too. My garden has different types of flowers.  I have roses in my garden. I have tulips in my garden. I have petunias in my garden.

text: He really likes it. Joe doesn't have enough money. Joe's dad tells him to keep saving his money.  Joe wants this car a lot. Joe asks his dad to help him. Joe and his dad make a deal. Japanese: He really likes it. / 彼はそれが本当に気に入りました。 Joe doesn't have enough money. / ジョーは十分なお金を持っていません。 Joe's dad tells him to keep saving his money. / ジョーの父親は、お金を貯め続けるように彼に言います。 Joe wants this car a lot. / ジョーはこの車がどうしても欲しいです。 Joe asks his dad to help him. / ジョーは父親に助けを求めます。 Joe and his dad make a deal. / ジョーと父親は約束を交わします。 Joe's dad will lend him the money. / ジョーの父親が彼にお金を貸してくれます。 Joe must work hard. / ジョーは一生懸命働かなければなりません。 He must pay the money back to his dad. / 彼は父親にお金を返さなければなりません。 Joe is very happy. / ジョーはとても幸せです。 Joe owns his first car. / ジョーは初めての自分の車を手に入れました。

explanation: rとaが繋がり「ｾﾞｱﾗｰ」になります。
text: Seasons. There are 4 seasons. Winter is the cold season. It snows in the winter. The winds blow, and ice forms on the water.  We play hockey on the ice. We play in the snow. After winter is the spring. That is when it begins to get warmer. Trees get buds on them. Flowers start to bloom. It rains a lot in the spring. Spring is followed by the summer.
Japanese: Seasons. / 季節。 There are 4 seasons. / 四季があります。 Winter is the cold season. / 冬は寒い季節です。 It snows in the winter. / 冬には雪が降ります。 The winds blow, and ice forms on the water. / 風が吹き、水面には氷が張ります。 We play hockey on the ice. / 私たちは氷の上でホッケーをします。 We play in the snow. / 私たちは雪の中で遊びます。 After winter is the spring. / 冬の次は春です。 That is when it begins to get warmer. / その頃から暖かくなり始めます。 Trees get buds on them. / 木々には芽が吹き始めます。 Flowers start to bloom. / 花が咲き始めます。 It rains a lot in the spring. / 春は雨がたくさん降ります。 Spring is followed by the summer. / 春の次は夏です。

explanation: tが母音に挟まれﾌﾗｯﾌﾟ化しつつ連結して「ﾎﾗｨﾝ」になります。
text: It can get very hot in the summertime. The sun shines brightly. we go swimming in the summer. we spend a lot of time outdoors. Many people go on vacations in the summer.  We get a summer vacation from school.  Summer is followed by the fall or autumn.  The leaves on the trees change colors. They change from green to red.  Orange and brown. The leaves fall off the trees. The weather gets cooler. The days get shorter. We go back to school in the fall, then winter comes again.  The seasons follow one after each other.
Japanese: It can get very hot in the summertime. / 夏の間は非常に暑くなることがあります。 The sun shines brightly. / 太陽が明るく輝きます。 we go swimming in the summer. / 私たちは夏に泳ぎに行きます。 we spend a lot of time outdoors. / 私たちは屋外で多くの時間を過ごします。 Many people go on vacations in the summer. / 夏には多くの人が休暇に出かけます。 We get a summer vacation from school. / 学校は夏休みになります。 Summer is followed by the fall or autumn. / 夏の次は秋です。 The leaves on the trees change colors. / 木々の葉が色づきます。 They change from green to red. / 緑から赤に変わります。 Orange and brown. The leaves fall off the trees. / オレンジ色や茶色に。葉が木から落ちます。 The weather gets cooler. / 天候が涼しくなります。 The days get shorter. / 日が短くなります。 We go back to school in the fall, then winter comes again. / 秋には学校に戻り、そしてまた冬がやってきます。 The seasons follow one after each other. / 季節は次から次へと巡ります。

Japanese: Ben and Melissa put ornaments on the tree. / ベンとメリッサはツリーにオーナメントを飾ります。 They put a star on top of the tree. / 彼らはツリーのてっぺんに星を飾ります。 They get ready for Santa Claus. / 彼らはサンタクロースを迎える準備をします。 They leave milk and cookies for Santa. / 彼らはサンタのためにミルクとクッキーを置いておきます。 Ben opens one present. / ベンはプレゼントを一つ開けます。 Melissa opens one present. / メリッサはプレゼントを一つ開けます。 They go to sleep. / 彼らは眠りにつきます。 Ben and Melissa wake up early. / ベンとメリッサは早く起きます。 They run downstairs. / 彼らは階下へ走っていきます。 There are a lot of presents under our tree. / ツリーの下にはたくさんのプレゼントがあります。 They wake up their mom and dad. / 彼らはお父さんとお母さんを起こします。 Ben and Melissa open their presents. / ベンとメリッサはプレゼントを開けます。 They love our presents. / 彼らは自分たちのプレゼントが大好きです。 Everyone cleans up the wrapping paper. / みんなで包装紙を片付けます。 It is time for breakfast. / 朝食の時間です。

Japanese: My dad makes pumpkin pies. / 父はパンプキンパイを作ります。 My mom cooks squash. / 母はカボチャを料理します。 I help my mom make stuffing. / 私は母がスタッフィング（詰め物）を作るのを手伝います。 I mix the bread and spices. / 私はパンとスパイスを混ぜます。 We make good stuffing. / 私たちは美味しいスタッフィングを作ります。 It goes inside the turkey. / それは七面鳥の中に入れます。 We put on nice clothes. / 私たちは素敵な服を着ます。 I set the table. / 私はテーブルをセットします。 My dad carves the turkey. / 父が七面鳥を切り分けます。 My mom makes the gravy. / 母がグレービーソースを作ります。 Our relatives arrive. / 親戚たちが到着します。 We say, thank you for all we have. / 私たちは、今あるすべてのものに感謝します。 We eat and eat. / 私たちはひたすら食べます。 It is a good Thanksgiving. / 良い感謝祭です。

Japanese: I love this jacket! Do you have another one just like it? このジャケット、とても気に入りました！これと全く同じものの在庫は他にありますか？  I’m afraid that’s the last one we have in medium. Is there something wrong with it? あいにく、Mサイズはこちらが最後の一点となっております。何か不具合がございましたでしょうか？  There’s a small tear on the seam of the left sleeve. But I really like it a lot! 左袖の縫い目のところに小さな破れがあるんです。でも、本当にこれが気に入っていて！  Well, if you buy the jacket as it is, then I can offer you a deal because of the tear. How does a 20% discount sound? さようですか。もし現状のままでご購入いただけるのでしたら、その破れがある分、お安くさせていただきます。20%引きというのはいかがでしょうか？

I can reserve a seat for you on the train departing for Edinburgh at ten-thirty. The cheapest fare is eighty-five pounds. OK, I'd like to buy that ticket, please. I’d also like to find out whether there's room on the train for passengers to take bicycles. Yes. there is, but it’ll cost three pounds more to reserve a space for your bike.
Japanese: Hi I’m calling to book a train ticket from London to Edinburgh, please. I’d like to leave around ten o’clock on Tuesday morning. こんにちは、ロンドンからエディンバラまでの列車のチケットを予約したくてお電話しました。火曜日の午前10時頃に出発したいのですが。  Certainly, sir. I can reserve a seat for you on the train departing for Edinburgh at ten-thirty. The cheapest fare is eighty-five pounds. かしこまりました。10時30分発のエディンバラ行きの列車の座席をご予約いただけます。最安の運賃は85ポンドでございます。  OK, I'd like to buy that ticket, please. I’d also like to find out whether there's room on the train for passengers to take bicycles. わかりました、そのチケットをお願いします。あわせて、列車内に自転車を持ち込めるスペースがあるかどうかも確認したいのですが。  Yes. there is, but it’ll cost three pounds more to reserve a space for your bike. はい、ございます。ただ、自転車用のスペースを予約するには、別途3ポンドが必要となります。

text: Excuse me, I’m sorry to disturb you - I came to use a library computer, but I forgot my library card and I can’t log on without it. Oh, that’s alright. Let me look up your account in our database. Do you have any other identification with you? Well, I have my driver’s license. It has my name and address on it. Okay, that's fine. I'll give you a password so you can access the computer now, but you won’t be able to use it after today.
Japanese: Excuse me, I’m sorry to disturb you - I came to use a library computer, but I forgot my library card and I can’t log on without it. すみません、お忙しいところ恐縮ですが、図書館のコンピュータを使おうと来たところ、図書カードを忘れてしまい、それがないとログインできないのです。  Oh, that’s alright. Let me look up your account in our database. Do you have any other identification with you? ああ、大丈夫ですよ。データベースでお客様のアカウントをお調べします。何か他に身分を証明できるものはお持ちでしょうか？  Well, I have my driver’s license. It has my name and address on it. ええと、運転免許証ならあります。名前と住所が記載されています。  Okay, that's fine. I'll give you a password so you can access the computer now, but you won’t be able to use it after today. はい、それで結構です。今コンピュータが使えるようにパスワードを発行しますが、明日以降は使えなくなりますので（今日限りとなりますので）ご了承ください。

explanation: kとoが繋がり「ﾊﾞｯｺﾝ」になります。
text: Thanks for meeting with me to revise the company budget, Georgia. No problem. With the increase in rent for office space this year, we’re definitely over budget right now. Yes, we’ll have to find areas to cut back on. What about parking? Mmm. It’s nice that we provide free passes to the nearest parking garage, but that does cost us a lot. Good point. We could split the cost and have employees pay half. That’d nearly cover the difference in rent.
Japanese: Thanks for meeting with me to revise the company budget, Georgia. ジョージア、会社の予算を修正するために打ち合わせの時間を作ってくれてありがとう。  No problem. いえいえ、もちろんです。  With the increase in rent for office space this year, we’re definitely over budget right now. 今年のオフィススペースの賃料値上げにより、現在は間違いなく予算を超過しています。  Yes, we’ll have to find areas to cut back on. What about parking? そうですね、どこか削減できる項目を見つける必要があります。駐車場についてはどうでしょうか？  Mmm. うーん、そうですね。  It’s nice that we provide free passes to the nearest parking garage, but that does cost us a lot. 最寄りの駐車場への無料パスを提供しているのは良いことなのですが、それにはかなりのコストがかかっています。  Good point. We could split the cost and have employees pay half. That’d nearly cover the difference in rent. 良い指摘ですね。費用を分担して、従業員に半分を負担してもらうこともできます。そうすれば、賃料の増額分をほぼ補えるでしょう。

We could split the cost and have employees pay half. That’d nearly cover the difference in rent. 良い指摘ですね。費用を分担して、従業員に半分を負担してもらうこともできます。そうすれば、賃料の増額分をほぼ補えるでしょう。

text: You know the music festival that’s starting this weekend? Um...someone mentioned it. Is it any good? It’s great! Mostly small folk groups, but they have well-known bands too. Some of us go every year - you want to join us? But. . . isn’t it too late to get tickets? Well, the way it works is...about six o’clock on Friday, people start lining up in the park. We all bring something to sit on-and food-so the wait’s not bad. As long as you’re at the park close to six, you’ll get a ticket-but you have to be there to get one. OK l think I can get there by six. Count me in!

Some of us go every year - you want to join us? 最高ですよ！ほとんどは小さなフォークグループですが、有名なバンドも出演します。私たちは毎年何人かで行っているのですが、あなたも一緒にどうですか？  But. . . isn’t it too late to get tickets? でも……チケットを入手するには、もう遅すぎるのではありませんか？  Well, the way it works is...about six o’clock on Friday, people start lining up in the park. We all bring something to sit on-and food-so the wait’s not bad. As long as you’re at the park close to six, you’ll get a ticket-but you have to be there to get one. ええと、仕組みはこうなんです。金曜日の午後6時頃に、公園でみんなが並び始めます。座るものや食べ物を持参するので、待ち時間はそんなに苦になりません。6時近くに公園にいればチケットは手に入りますが、本人がその場にいないといけないんですよ。  OK l think I can get there by six. Count me in! わかりました、6時までには行けると思います。ぜひ私も仲間にいれてください！

explanation: ngとaが繋がり「ｵｰﾌﾟﾆﾝｶﾞ」になります。
text: Hi, I’m Eddy Burgess. I’m opening a new law firm and I’m looking for ways to advertise it. Your company designed a Web site for my friend’s jewelry store, and she suggested I contact you. Thanks for calling, Mr. Burgess. This is a great way to advertise your business. Our experienced consultants can design a Web site that will help you to stand out from other firms. That’s great! Could I set up an appointment for tomorrow to discuss ideas with a consultant? I think so. I’ll transfer you to one, but before I do that, could you tell me the name of your friend? We like to thank people who recommend our services.
Japanese: Hi, I’m Eddy Burgess. I’m opening a new law firm and I’m looking for ways to advertise it. Your company designed a Web site for my friend’s jewelry store, and she suggested I contact you. こんにちは、エディ・バージェスです。新しく法律事務所を開設する予定で、その宣伝方法を探しています。貴社が私の友人の宝石店のウェブサイトを制作されたそうで、彼女から連絡してみるよう勧められました。  Thanks for calling, Mr. Burgess. This is a great way to advertise your business. Our experienced consultants can design a Web site that will help you to stand out from other firms. バージェス様、お電話ありがとうございます。ウェブサイトはビジネスを宣伝する上で非常に優れた手段です。弊社の経験豊富なコンサルタントが、他の事務所との差別化を図れるようなウェブサイトをデザインいたします。  That’s great! Could I set up an appointment for tomorrow to discuss ideas with a consultant? それは素晴らしいですね！明日、コンサルタントの方とアイデアを相談するための予約を入れることはできますか？  I think so. I’ll transfer you to one, but before I do that, could you tell me the name of your friend? We like to thank people who recommend our services. はい、可能だと思います。担当者にお繋ぎいたしますが、その前にご紹介いただいたご友人のお名前を教えていただけますでしょうか？弊社のサービスを推薦してくださった方には、ぜひお礼を申し上げたいと考えております。

Thanks for calling, Mr. Burgess. This is a great way to advertise your business. Our experienced consultants can design a Web site that will help you to stand out from other firms. That’s great! Could I set up an appointment for tomorrow to discuss ideas with a consultant? I think so. I’ll transfer you to one, but before I do that, could you tell me the name of your friend? We like to thank people who recommend our services.
Japanese: Hi, I’m Eddy Burgess. I’m opening a new law firm and I’m looking for ways to advertise it. Your company designed a Web site for my friend’s jewelry store, and she suggested I contact you. こんにちは、エディ・バージェスです。新しく法律事務所を開設する予定で、その宣伝方法を探しています。貴社が私の友人の宝石店のウェブサイトを制作されたそうで、彼女から連絡してみるよう勧められました。  Thanks for calling, Mr. Burgess. This is a great way to advertise your business. Our experienced consultants can design a Web site that will help you to stand out from other firms. バージェス様、お電話ありがとうございます。ウェブサイトはビジネスを宣伝する上で非常に優れた手段です。弊社の経験豊富なコンサルタントが、他の事務所との差別化を図れるようなウェブサイトをデザインいたします。  That’s great! Could I set up an appointment for tomorrow to discuss ideas with a consultant? それは素晴らしいですね！明日、コンサルタントの方とアイデアを相談するための予約を入れることはできますか？  I think so. I’ll transfer you to one, but before I do that, could you tell me the name of your friend? We like to thank people who recommend our services. はい、可能だと思います。担当者にお繋ぎいたしますが、その前にご紹介いただいたご友人のお名前を教えていただけますでしょうか？弊社のサービスを推薦してくださった方には、ぜひお礼を申し上げたいと考えております。

explanation: mとiが繋がり「ﾈｲﾐｽﾞ」になります。
text: Hi-my name’s Tom Wilson. I’m visiting family here in Miami for three months, and I’m hoping to earn a bit of money while I’m in town. Does your restaurant happen to have any short-term job openings? Actually, I think we could use some help in the kitchen, since it’s the beginning of the tourist season now. But do you have any restaurant experience? Um, I worked for a couple of years in a French restaurant in New Orleans. I’m sure the manager there would give me a recommendation. OK That sounds promising. If you wait here a second, I’m going to go to the kitchen to see if our chef’s available to talk with you.
Japanese: Hi-my name’s Tom Wilson. I’m visiting family here in Miami for three months, and I’m hoping to earn a bit of money while I’m in town. Does your restaurant happen to have any short-term job openings? こんにちは、トム・ウィルソンと申します。3ヶ月間、ここマイアミの家族の元に滞在しているのですが、滞在中に少しお金を稼げればと考えています。こちらのレストランで、もしや短期の求人募集などはございませんか？  Actually, I think we could use some help in the kitchen, since it’s the beginning of the tourist season now. But do you have any restaurant experience? そうですね、今はちょうど観光シーズンの始まりですので、厨房で人手が必要かもしれません。ですが、レストランでの勤務経験はありますか？  Um, I worked for a couple of years in a French restaurant in New Orleans. I’m sure the manager there would give me a recommendation. ええと、ニューオーリンズのフランス料理店で2年ほど働いていました。そこのマネージャーなら、きっと私のことを推薦してくれるはずです。  OK That sounds promising. If you wait here a second, I’m going to go to the kitchen to see if our chef’s available to talk with you. わかりました。それは期待できそうですね。少々ここでお待ちいただければ、シェフが今お話しできる状況かどうか、厨房へ確認してまいります。

explanation: tとaが繋がり､さらにnとeが繋がって「ｶﾞﾗﾈｨﾒｰﾙ」になります｡
text: Hi, Girolamo. I just got an e-mail from T-H-Y Incorporated. They’re wondering when we can begin building their new headquarters. They want a start date. I’ve been meaning to contact them, but I’m waiting to confirm some results from the soil analysis. There’s a patch of ground that might be too moist and unstable to build on. Our analysts are doing a few more tests. Yeah, it’s probably best to wait for those results to come in before setting a definite date. In the meantime, I’ll write back and let T-H-Y know what’s going on.
Japanese: Hi, Girolamo. I just got an e-mail from T-H-Y Incorporated. They’re wondering when we can begin building their new headquarters. They want a start date. こんにちは、ジローラモ。T-H-Y社からメールが届きました。彼らはいつ新本社の建設を開始できるか知りたがっています。具体的な着工日の提示を求めています。  I’ve been meaning to contact them, but I’m waiting to confirm some results from the soil analysis. There’s a patch of ground that might be too moist and unstable to build on. Our analysts are doing a few more tests. こちらからも連絡しようと思っていたのですが、土壌調査の結果確認を待っているところなのです。建設するには湿り気が多すぎて、地盤が不安定かもしれない箇所があります。現在、分析担当者がいくつか追加のテストを行っています。  Yeah, it’s probably best to wait for those results to come in before setting a definite date. In the meantime, I’ll write back and let T-H-Y know what’s going on. そうですね、具体的な日程を決める前に、調査結果が出るのを待つのがおそらく最善でしょう。とりあえず、私から返信して、T-H-Y社に現在の状況を伝えておきます。

I’ve been meaning to contact them, but I’m waiting to confirm some results from the soil analysis. There’s a patch of ground that might be too moist and unstable to build on. Our analysts are doing a few more tests. Yeah, it’s probably best to wait for those results to come in before setting a definite date. In the meantime, I’ll write back and let T-H-Y know what’s going on.
Japanese: Hi, Girolamo. I just got an e-mail from T-H-Y Incorporated. They’re wondering when we can begin building their new headquarters. They want a start date. こんにちは、ジローラモ。T-H-Y社からメールが届きました。彼らはいつ新本社の建設を開始できるか知りたがっています。具体的な着工日の提示を求めています。  I’ve been meaning to contact them, but I’m waiting to confirm some results from the soil analysis. There’s a patch of ground that might be too moist and unstable to build on. Our analysts are doing a few more tests. こちらからも連絡しようと思っていたのですが、土壌調査の結果確認を待っているところなのです。建設するには湿り気が多すぎて、地盤が不安定かもしれない箇所があります。現在、分析担当者がいくつか追加のテストを行っています。  Yeah, it’s probably best to wait for those results to come in before setting a definite date. In the meantime, I’ll write back and let T-H-Y know what’s going on. そうですね、具体的な日程を決める前に、調査結果が出るのを待つのがおそらく最善でしょう。とりあえず、私から返信して、T-H-Y社に現在の状況を伝えておきます。

text: I can’t believe the Vancouver convention’s so soon! I know. It should be great, with all those big-name speakers. Are we all set for transportation? Uh- the plane tickets are already taken care of, and you arranged for the rental car while we’re there-right, Mike? Uh-oh. I completely forgot to make the car reservation! I’ve been so busy getting my workshop materials ready! All right... We’re only two days away from leaving-it might be hard to get a car now! Let’s not stress out about it. I saw something about a bus service to and from the hotel. Let’s talk to the hotel receptionist and see.
Japanese: I can’t believe the Vancouver convention’s so soon! バンクーバーの会議（コンベンション）がもうすぐだなんて信じられません！  I know. It should be great, with all those big-name speakers. Are we all set for transportation? 本当ですね。あんなに有名な講師陣が揃うのですから、素晴らしいものになるはずです。移動手段の準備は万端ですか？  Uh- the plane tickets are already taken care of, and you arranged for the rental car while we’re there-right, Mike? ええと、航空券の手配はすでに済んでいます。滞在中のレンタカーは君が手配してくれたんだよね、マイク？  Uh-oh. I completely forgot to make the car reservation! I’ve been so busy getting my workshop materials ready! おっと。車の予約をするのを完全に忘れていました！ワークショップの資料を準備するのに必死だったんです！  All right... We’re only two days away from leaving-it might be hard to get a car now! 困りましたね……出発まであと2日しかありません。今から車を確保するのは難しいかもしれませんよ！  Let’s not stress out about it. I saw something about a bus service to and from the hotel. Let’s talk to the hotel receptionist and see. あまり焦らないようにしましょう。ホテル送迎のバスサービスについて何かで見かけました。ホテルの受付に相談して、確認してみましょう。

explanation: ngとiが繋がり「ﾁｪｯｷﾝｷﾞﾝ」になります。
text: Hi. Barry. I’m just checking in. How’s everything going up here? Are you finished cleaning the Romano Construction offices yet? No, it’s taking longer than expected. I vacuumed the carpet, but there are a lot of stains. So I decided to shampoo it. But then I had to go down and get the steam-cleaning machine and bring it up here. Well, before you start shampooing, could you come downstairs? I need some help moving a big table in one of the conference rooms. Sure, I’ll be right down. This is a good time for me to take a break from these carpets, anyway.
Japanese: Hi. Barry. I’m just checking in. How’s everything going up here? Are you finished cleaning the Romano Construction offices yet? こんにちは、バリー。様子を見に来ました。こちらの状況はどうですか？ロマーノ建設のオフィスの清掃はもう終わりましたか？  No, it’s taking longer than expected. I vacuumed the carpet, but there are a lot of stains. So I decided to shampoo it. But then I had to go down and get the steam-cleaning machine and bring it up here. いえ、思ったより時間がかかっています。カーペットに掃除機をかけたのですが、汚れがひどくて。それでシャンプー洗浄をすることにしたんです。ただ、下の階までスチームクリーナーを取りに行って、ここまで運んでこなければなりませんでした。  Well, before you start shampooing, could you come downstairs? I need some help moving a big table in one of the conference rooms. そうですか。では、シャンプー洗浄を始める前に、下の階に来てもらえませんか？会議室の一つの大きなテーブルを動かすのを手伝ってほしいのです。  Sure, I’ll be right down. This is a good time for me to take a break from these carpets, anyway. 承知いたしました、すぐに行きます。どちらにせよ、ちょうどカーペット掃除の休憩を取りたいと思っていたところだったので。

explanation: nとIが繋がり「ｳｪﾅｲ」になります。
text: When I was 27 years old, I left a very demanding job in management consulting for a job that was even more demanding: teaching. I went to teach seventh graders math in the New York City public schools. And like any teacher, I made quizzes and tests. I gave out homework assignments. When the work came back, I calculated grades. What struck me was that IQ was not the only difference between my best and my worst students. Some of my strongest performers did not have stratospheric IQ scores. Some of my smartest kids weren't doing so well.
Japanese: When I was 27 years old, I left a very demanding job in management consulting for a job that was even more demanding: teaching. 27歳の時、私は経営コンサルティングという非常にハードな仕事を辞め、それよりもさらにハードな仕事に就きました。それは「教えること（教師）」です。  I went to teach seventh graders math in the New York City public schools. ニューヨーク市の公立学校で、中学1年生（7年生）に数学を教えに行きました。  And like any teacher, I made quizzes and tests. I gave out homework assignments. When the work came back, I calculated grades. そして、他のどの教師とも同じように、小テストや試験を作り、宿題を出し、提出された課題を採点して成績を算出しました。  What struck me was that IQ was not the only difference between my best and my worst students. そこで私が衝撃を受けたのは、成績が最も良い生徒と最も悪い生徒との間にあった違いは、決してIQ（知能指数）だけではないということでした。  Some of my strongest performers did not have stratospheric IQ scores. 非常に優秀な成績を収めている生徒の中には、驚くほど高いIQスコアを持っていない子もいました。  Some of my smartest kids weren't doing so well. その一方で、いわゆる「最も頭が良い」はずの子供たちの中に、あまり成績が振るわない子がいたのです。

When the work came back, I calculated grades. そして、他のどの教師とも同じように、小テストや試験を作り、宿題を出し、提出された課題を採点して成績を算出しました。  What struck me was that IQ was not the only difference between my best and my worst students. そこで私が衝撃を受けたのは、成績が最も良い生徒と最も悪い生徒との間にあった違いは、決してIQ（知能指数）だけではないということでした。  Some of my strongest performers did not have stratospheric IQ scores. 非常に優秀な成績を収めている生徒の中には、驚くほど高いIQスコアを持っていない子もいました。  Some of my smartest kids weren't doing so well. その一方で、いわゆる「最も頭が良い」はずの子供たちの中に、あまり成績が振るわない子がいたのです。

text: And that got me thinking. The kinds of things you need to learn in seventh grade math, sure, they're hard: ratios, decimals, the area of a parallelogram. But these concepts are not impossible, and I was firmly convinced that every one of my students could learn the material if they worked hard and long enough.
Japanese: And that got me thinking. そこで、私は考えさせられました。  The kinds of things you need to learn in seventh grade math, sure, they're hard: ratios, decimals, the area of a parallelogram. 中学1年生（7年生）の数学で学ぶ内容は、確かに難しいものです。比率や小数、平行四辺形の面積などです。  But these concepts are not impossible, and I was firmly convinced that every one of my students could learn the material if they worked hard and long enough. しかし、これらの概念は決して理解不可能なものではありません。生徒たちが十分に時間をかけ、懸命に努力しさえすれば、一人残らずこの学習内容を習得できるはずだと、私は固く信じていました。

text: After several more years of teaching, I came to the conclusion that what we need in education is a much better understanding of students and learning from a motivational perspective, from a psychological perspective. In education, the one thing we know how to measure best is IQ. But what if doing well in school and in life depends on much more than your ability to learn quickly and easily?
Japanese: After several more years of teaching, I came to the conclusion that what we need in education is a much better understanding of students and learning from a motivational perspective, from a psychological perspective. さらに数年間教師として経験を積んだ後、私はある結論に達しました。教育において本当に必要なのは、動機づけの観点や心理学的な観点から、生徒や学習というものをより深く理解することであるということです。  In education, the one thing we know how to measure best is IQ. 現在の教育において、私たちが最も正確に測定する方法を知っている唯一のものはIQ（知能指数）です。  But what if doing well in school and in life depends on much more than your ability to learn quickly and easily? ですが、もし学校や人生で成功を収めることが、素早く簡単に学習できる能力以上に、もっと別の多くの要素に左右されるとしたらどうでしょうか？

text: So I left the classroom, and I went to graduate school to become a psychologist. I started studying kids and adults in all kinds of super challenging settings, and in every study my question was, who is successful here and why? My research team and I went to West Point Military Academy. We tried to predict which cadets would stay in military training and which would drop out. We went to the National Spelling Bee and tried to predict which children would advance farthest in competition.
Japanese: So I left the classroom, and I went to graduate school to become a psychologist. そこで私は教壇を離れ、心理学者になるために大学院へ進みました。  I started studying kids and adults in all kinds of super challenging settings, and in every study my question was, who is successful here and why? 私は、ありとあらゆる非常に過酷な環境にいる子供や大人を対象に研究を始めました。そしてどの調査においても私の問いは、「ここで成功を収めるのは誰か、そしてそれはなぜか？」というものでした。  My research team and I went to West Point Military Academy. 私の研究チームと私は、ウエストポイント陸軍士官学校へ赴きました。  We tried to predict which cadets would stay in military training and which would drop out. 私たちは、どの候補生が軍事訓練に残り、どの候補生が脱落するかを予測しようとしました。  We went to the National Spelling Bee and tried to predict which children would advance farthest in competition. 私たちは全米スペリング・ビー大会にも足を運び、どの子供が大会で最も上位まで勝ち進むかを予測しようとしました。

text: We studied rookie teachers working in really tough neighborhoods, asking which teachers are still going to be here in teaching by the end of the school year, and of those, who will be the most effective at improving learning outcomes for their students? We partnered with private companies, asking, which of these salespeople is going to keep their jobs? And who's going to earn the most money?
Japanese: We studied rookie teachers working in really tough neighborhoods, asking which teachers are still going to be here in teaching by the end of the school year, and of those, who will be the most effective at improving learning outcomes for their students? 私たちは非常に困難な環境にある地域で働く新人教師たちを対象に、学年末まで誰が教師を続けているか、そしてその中で、誰が生徒の学習成果を向上させるのに最も効果的であるかを調査しました。  We partnered with private companies, asking, which of these salespeople is going to keep their jobs? 私たちは民間企業とも提携し、これらの販売員のうち誰が職に留まるのかを調査しました。  And who's going to earn the most money? そして、誰が最も多くの収入を得るのでしょうか？

text: In all those very different contexts, one characteristic emerged as a significant predictor of success. And it wasn't social intelligence. It wasn't good looks, physical health, and it wasn't IQ. It was grit. Grit is passion and perseverance for very long-term goals. Grit is having stamina. Grit is sticking with your future, day in, day out, not just for the week, not just for the month, but for years, and working really hard to make that future a reality. Grit is living life like it's a marathon, not a sprint.
Japanese: In all those very different contexts, one characteristic emerged as a significant predictor of success. これらすべての非常に異なる状況において、ある一つの特性が成功の重要な予測因子として浮上しました。  And it wasn't social intelligence. It wasn't good looks, physical health, and it wasn't IQ. It was grit. そしてそれは、社会的知性ではありませんでした。外見の良さでも、身体的な健康でも、そしてIQでもありませんでした。それは「グリット（やり抜く力）」でした。  Grit is passion and perseverance for very long-term goals. グリットとは、非常に長期的な目標に向けた情熱と粘り強さのことです。  Grit is having stamina. Grit is sticking with your future, day in, day out, not just for the week, not just for the month, but for years, and working really hard to make that future a reality. グリットとはスタミナを持つことです。グリットとは、自分の将来を信じ抜くことです。来る日も来る日も、単に一週間や一ヶ月だけでなく、何年もの間。そして、その未来を現実にするために懸命に努力し続けることなのです。  Grit is living life like it's a marathon, not a sprint. グリットとは、人生を短距離走ではなく、マラソンのように生きることです。

explanation: s(z音)とaが繋がり「ｲｱｰｽﾞｧｺﾞｳ」になります。
text: A few years ago, I started studying grit in the Chicago public schools. I asked thousands of high school juniors to take grit questionnaires, and then waited around more than a year to see who would graduate. Turns out that grittier kids were significantly more likely to graduate, even when I matched them on every characteristic I could measure, things like family income, standardized achievement test scores, even how safe kids felt when they were at school.
Japanese: A few years ago, I started studying grit in the Chicago public schools. 数年前、私はシカゴの公立学校で「グリット（やり抜く力）」の研究を始めました。  I asked thousands of high school juniors to take grit questionnaires, and then waited around more than a year to see who would graduate. 数千人の高校2年生（11年生）にグリットに関するアンケート調査への回答を依頼し、その後1年以上かけて、誰が実際に卒業するのかを追跡調査しました。  Turns out that grittier kids were significantly more likely to graduate, even when I matched them on every characteristic I could measure, things like family income, standardized achievement test scores, even how safe kids felt when they were at school. その結果、グリットがより強い子供たちは、卒業できる可能性が極めて高いことが判明しました。たとえ世帯収入や標準学力テストのスコア、さらには学校でどれほど安全に感じているかといった、測定可能なあらゆる特性を等しくして比較しても、その差は歴然でした。

explanation: tとaが繋がり「ｼﾞｬｽﾀｯ」になります。
text: So it's not just at West Point or the National Spelling Bee that grit matters. It's also in school, especially for kids at risk for dropping out. To me, the most shocking thing about grit is how little we know, how little science knows, about building it. Every day, parents and teachers ask me, "How do I build grit in kids? What do I do to teach kids a solid work ethic? How do I keep them motivated for the long run?" The honest answer is, I don't know.
Japanese: So it's not just at West Point or the National Spelling Bee that grit matters. It's also in school, especially for kids at risk for dropping out. つまり、グリットが重要となるのは、ウエストポイント（陸軍士官学校）や全米スペリング・ビーのような場だけではありません。学校においても、特に中退の危機にある子供たちにとって重要なのです。  To me, the most shocking thing about grit is how little we know, how little science knows, about building it. 私にとって、グリットについて最も衝撃的なのは、それをどう育むかについて、私たち、そして科学がいかに何も知らないかということです。  Every day, parents and teachers ask me, "How do I build grit in kids? What do I do to teach kids a solid work ethic? 毎日、保護者や教師の方々からこう聞かれます。「どうすれば子供たちのグリットを育てられますか？ 子供たちにしっかりとした勤労意欲（ワークエシック）を教えるには何をすればいいですか？」  How do I keep them motivated for the long run?" The honest answer is, I don't know. 「どうすれば彼らの意欲を長期にわたって維持できるでしょうか？」と。正直な答えを言えば、私にも分からないのです。

What do I do to teach kids a solid work ethic? How do I keep them motivated for the long run?" The honest answer is, I don't know.
Japanese: So it's not just at West Point or the National Spelling Bee that grit matters. It's also in school, especially for kids at risk for dropping out. つまり、グリットが重要となるのは、ウエストポイント（陸軍士官学校）や全米スペリング・ビーのような場だけではありません。学校においても、特に中退の危機にある子供たちにとって重要なのです。  To me, the most shocking thing about grit is how little we know, how little science knows, about building it. 私にとって、グリットについて最も衝撃的なのは、それをどう育むかについて、私たち、そして科学がいかに何も知らないかということです。  Every day, parents and teachers ask me, "How do I build grit in kids? What do I do to teach kids a solid work ethic? 毎日、保護者や教師の方々からこう聞かれます。「どうすれば子供たちのグリットを育てられますか？ 子供たちにしっかりとした勤労意欲（ワークエシック）を教えるには何をすればいいですか？」  How do I keep them motivated for the long run?" The honest answer is, I don't know. 「どうすれば彼らの意欲を長期にわたって維持できるでしょうか？」と。正直な答えを言えば、私にも分からないのです。

text: What I do know is that talent doesn't make you gritty. Our data show very clearly that there are many talented individuals who simply do not follow through on their commitments. In fact, in our data, grit is usually unrelated or even inversely related to measures of talent. So far, the best idea I've heard about building grit in kids is something called "growth mindset.” This is an idea developed at Stanford University by Carol Dweck, and it is the belief that the ability to learn is not fixed, that it can change with your effort.
Japanese: What I do know is that talent doesn't make you gritty. 私が分かっているのは、才能があるからといって「やり抜く力（グリット）」が備わるわけではないということです。  Our data show very clearly that there are many talented individuals who simply do not follow through on their commitments. 私たちのデータは、才能に恵まれていながら、自分が決めたことを最後までやり遂げられない個人が数多く存在することを非常に明確に示しています。  In fact, in our data, grit is usually unrelated or even inversely related to measures of talent. 実際、私たちのデータでは、やり抜く力は通常、才能の指標とは無関係であるか、あるいは負の相関（反比例）関係にさえあります。  So far, the best idea I've heard about building grit in kids is something called "growth mindset.” これまでのところ、子供たちのやり抜く力を育むための最良のアイデアとして私が耳にしたのは、「成長マインドセット」と呼ばれるものです。  This is an idea developed at Stanford University by Carol Dweck, and it is the belief that the ability to learn is not fixed, that it can change with your effort. これはスタンフォード大学のキャロル・ドゥエック教授によって提唱された考え方で、学習能力は固定されたものではなく、努力によって変えることができるという信念のことです。

text: A few years ago, I felt like I was stuck in a rut, so I decided to follow in the footsteps of the great American philosopher, Morgan Spurlock, and try something new for 30 days. The idea is actually pretty simple. Think about something you've always wanted to add to your life and try it for the next 30days. It turns out 30 days is just about the right amount of time to add a new habit or subtract a habit like watching the news from your life.
Japanese: A few years ago, I felt like I was stuck in a rut, so I decided to follow in the footsteps of the great American philosopher, Morgan Spurlock, and try something new for 30 days. 数年前、私は自分がマンネリに陥っている（型にはまって身動きが取れなくなっている）と感じたので、偉大なアメリカの哲学者モーガン・スパーロックの足跡をたどり、30日間何か新しいことに挑戦してみることにしました。  The idea is actually pretty simple. そのアイデアは、実は至ってシンプルなものです。  Think about something you've always wanted to add to your life and try it for the next 30days. これまでの人生にずっと加えたいと思っていたことを何か一つ考え、それを次の30日間、実際に試してみるのです。  It turns out 30 days is just about the right amount of time to add a new habit or subtract a habit like watching the news from your life. 30日間というのは、新しい習慣を身につけたり、あるいはニュースを見るといった習慣を人生から取り除いたりするのに、ちょうど良い長さであることがわかりました。

text: There's a few things that I learned while doing these 30-day challenges. The first was, instead of the months flying by, forgotten, the time was much more memorable. This was part of a challenge I did to take a picture every day for a month. And I remember exactly where I was and what I was doing that day. I also noticed that as I started to do more and harder 30-day challenges, my self- confidence grew. I went from desk-dwelling computer nerd to the kind of guy who bikes to work. For fun.
Japanese: There's a few things that I learned while doing these 30-day challenges. この30日間チャレンジに取り組む中で、いくつか学んだことがあります。  The first was, instead of the months flying by, forgotten, the time was much more memorable. ひとつ目は、ただ月日が過ぎ去り忘れられてしまう代わりに、その時間がずっと記憶に残るものになったことです。  This was part of a challenge I did to take a picture every day for a month. And I remember exactly where I was and what I was doing that day. これは、1ヶ月間毎日写真を撮るというチャレンジの一環でした。その日、自分がどこにいて何をしていたのかを、今でも正確に覚えています。  I also noticed that as I started to do more and harder 30-day challenges, my self- confidence grew. また、より多くの、そしてより困難な30日間チャレンジに挑戦し始めるにつれて、自分に自信がついてきたことにも気づきました。  I went from desk-dwelling computer nerd to the kind of guy who bikes to work. For fun. 私は、机にかじりついてばかりのコンピュータ・オタクから、自転車で通勤するような男へと変わりました。それも、楽しみのために（好きで）やっているのです。

text: Even last year, I ended up hiking up Mt. Kilimanjaro, the highest mountain in Africa. I would never have been that adventurous before I started my 30-day challenges. I also figured out that if you really want something badly enough, you can do anything for 30 days. Have you ever wanted to write a novel? Every November, tens of thousands of people try to write their own 50,000-word novel, from scratch, in 30 days. It turns out, all you have to do is write 1,667 words a day for a month. So, I did. By the way, the secret is not to go to sleep until you've written your words for the day. You might be sleep-deprived, but you'll finish your novel. Now is my book the next great American novel? No. I wrote it in a month. It's awful.
Japanese: Even last year, I ended up hiking up Mt. Kilimanjaro, the highest mountain in Africa. I would never have been that adventurous before I started my 30-day challenges. 昨年は、アフリカ最高峰のキリマンジャロに登ることまでしました。30日間チャレンジを始める前の私なら、これほど冒険的になることは決してなかったでしょう。  I also figured out that if you really want something badly enough, you can do anything for 30 days. また、何かを本当に心から望んでいるのであれば、30日間なら何だってできるということも分かりました。  Have you ever wanted to write a novel? 皆さんは、小説を書きたいと思ったことはありませんか？  Every November, tens of thousands of people try to write their own 50,000-word novel, from scratch, in 30 days. 毎年11月には、何万人もの人々が、30日間で5万語の小説をゼロから書き上げようと挑戦しています。  It turns out, all you have to do is write 1,667 words a day for a month. So, I did. By the way, the secret is not to go to sleep until you've written your words for the day. 結局のところ、1ヶ月間、毎日1,667語を書きさえすれば良いのです。ですから、私もやりました。ちなみに、秘訣はその日のノルマを書き終えるまで寝ないことです。  You might be sleep-deprived, but you'll finish your novel. Now is my book the next great American novel? 睡眠不足になるかもしれませんが、小説を完成させることはできます。さて、私の書いた本は、アメリカ文学の歴史に残るような次なる傑作でしょうか？  No. I wrote it in a month. It's awful. いいえ、とんでもない。たった1ヶ月で書いたのですから。ひどい出来ですよ。

explanation: s(z音)とaが繋がり「ｲｱｰｽﾞｧｺﾞｳ」になります。
text: Seven years ago, a student came to me and asked me to invest in his company. He said, "I'm working with three friends, and we're going to try to disrupt an industry by selling stuff online. " And I said, "OK, you guys spent the whole summer on this, right?" "No, we all took internships just in case it doesn't work out." “All right, but you're going to go in full time once you graduate.” “Not exactly. We’ve all lined up backup jobs.” Six months go by, it's the day before the company launches, and there is still not a functioning website. "You guys realize, the entire company is a website. That's literally all it is." So I obviously declined to invest. And they ended up naming the company Warby Parker. They sell glasses online. They were recently recognized as the world's most innovative company and valued at over a billion dollars. And now? My wife handles our investments. Why was I so wrong?

text: To find out, I've been studying people that I come to call "originals." Originals are nonconformists, people who not only have new ideas but take action to champion them. They are people who stand out and speak up. Originals drive creativity and change in the world. They're the people you want to bet on. And they look nothing like I expected. I want to show you today three things I've learned about recognizing originals and becoming a little bit more like them. So the first reason that I passed on Warby Parker was they were really slow getting off the ground. Now, you are all intimately familiar with the mind of a procrastinator. Well, I have a confession for you. I'm the opposite. I'm a precrastinator. Yes, that's an actual term. You know that panic you feel a few hours before a big deadline when you haven't done anything yet. I just feel that a few months ahead of time.
Japanese: ご要望に合わせて、アダム・グラント氏の「オリジナルズ」に関するスピーチの続きを、各英文のすぐ下に日本語訳を配置する形式で作成しました。  「Precrastinator（前倒しにする人）」という、彼独自のユニークな表現や専門用語を、文脈に合わせて丁寧に翻訳しています。  To find out, I've been studying people that I come to call "originals." その理由を突き止めるために、私は「オリジナルズ（独創的な人たち）」と呼ぶようになった人々を研究してきました。  Originals are nonconformists, people who not only have new ideas but take action to champion them. They are people who stand out and speak up. Originals drive creativity and change in the world. They're the people you want to bet on. And they look nothing like I expected. オリジナルズとは非順応主義者のことで、単に新しいアイデアを持つだけでなく、それを擁護し実現するために行動を起こす人々のことです。彼らは周囲に埋もれず、声を上げる人々です。オリジナルズは世界の創造性と変化を推し進めます。彼らこそ、賭ける（投資する）価値のある人々です。そして、彼らの実像は、私の予想とは全く異なるものでした。  I want to show you today three things I've learned about recognizing originals and becoming a little bit more like them. 今日は、オリジナルズを見極め、自分自身も少しだけ彼らに近づくために私が学んだ3つのことをお話ししたいと思います。  So the first reason that I passed on Warby Parker was they were really slow getting off the ground. Now, you are all intimately familiar with the mind of a procrastinator. Well, I have a confession for you. I'm the opposite. I'm a precrastinator. さて、私がワービー・パーカーへの投資を見送った最初の理由は、彼らの事業の立ち上げが非常に遅かったことです。さて、皆さんは「先延ばしにする人（プロクラスティネーター）」の心理をよくご存知でしょう。ここで皆さんに告白があります。私はその正反対なのです。私は「前倒しにする人（プリクラスティネーター）」です。  Yes, that's an actual term. You know that panic you feel a few hours before a big deadline when you haven't done anything yet. I just feel that a few months ahead of time. はい、これは実際に使われている用語です。大きな締め切りの数時間前、まだ何も手をつけていない時に感じるあのパニックを、皆さんもご存知でしょう。私はそれを数ヶ月前から感じてしまうのです。

explanation: tが母音に挟まれﾌﾗｯﾌﾟ化し､earlyと繋がって「ｽﾀｰﾘｯﾀﾞｰﾘｰ」になります｡
text: So this started early: when I was a kid, I took Nintendo games very seriously. I would wake up at 5am, start playing and not stop until I had mastered them. Eventually it got so out of hand that a local newspaper came and did a story on the dark side of Nintendo, starring me. But this served me well in college, because I finished my senior thesis four months before the deadline. And I was proud of that, until a few years ago. I had a student named Jihae, who came to me and said, "I have my most creative ideas when I'm procrastinating." And I was like, "That's cute, where are the four papers you owe me.
Japanese: So this started early: when I was a kid, I took Nintendo games very seriously. こうした傾向は早くから始まりました。子供の頃、私は任天堂のゲームにとても真剣に取り組んでいました。  I would wake up at 5am, start playing and not stop until I had mastered them. Eventually it got so out of hand that a local newspaper came and did a story on the dark side of Nintendo, starring me. 朝の5時に起きてプレイを始め、全クリアするまで止めませんでした。最終的には手に負えないほどになり、地元の新聞が「任天堂のダークサイド（負の側面）」という特集を組み、私がその主役として取り上げられたほどです。  But this served me well in college, because I finished my senior thesis four months before the deadline. しかし、この性質は大学時代には役に立ちました。卒業論文を締め切りの4ヶ月前に完成させたからです。  And I was proud of that, until a few years ago. I had a student named Jihae, who came to me and said, "I have my most creative ideas when I'm procrastinating." And I was like, "That's cute, where are the four papers you owe me." 数年前までは、そのことを誇りに思っていました。ところが、ジヘという学生が私のところへ来て、こう言いました。「私は、物事を先延ばしにしている時にこそ、最も独創的なアイデアが浮かぶんです」 私はこう返しました。「それはいい話だね。ところで、君が提出すべき4本のレポートはどこにあるんだい？」

text: No, she was one of our most creative students, and as an organizational psychologist, this is the kind of idea that I test. So I challenged her she gets some data. She goes into a bunch of companies. She has people fill out surveys about how often they procrastinate. Then she gets their bosses to rate how creative and innovative they are. And sure enough, the precrastinators like me, who rush in and do everything early are rated as less creative than people who procrastinate moderately. So I want to know what happens to the chronic procrastinators. She was like, "I don't know. They didn't fill out my survey.”
Japanese: No, she was one of our most creative students, and as an organizational psychologist, this is the kind of idea that I test. So I challenged her she gets some data. She goes into a bunch of companies. She has people fill out surveys about how often they procrastinate. Then she gets their bosses to rate how creative and innovative they are. いえ、彼女は私たちの教え子の中でも最も独創的な学生の一人でした。そして、組織心理学者として、これこそが私が検証すべき種類のアイデアだったのです。そこで私は彼女にデータを集めてくるよう促しました。彼女は多くの企業に赴き、従業員に「どの程度物事を先延ばしにするか」というアンケートに回答してもらいました。その後、彼らの上司に、部下がいかに独創的で革新的であるかを評価してもらったのです。  And sure enough, the precrastinators like me, who rush in and do everything early are rated as less creative than people who procrastinate moderately. So I want to know what happens to the chronic procrastinators. 案の定、私のように何でも早めに済ませようと急ぐ「前倒し派（プリクラスティネーター）」は、適度に先延ばしをする人々よりも創造性が低いと評価されました。そこで私は、慢性的に先延ばしをする人々はどうなるのかを知りたいと思いました。  She was like, "I don't know. They didn't fill out my survey.” すると彼女はこう言ったのです。「分かりません。彼らはアンケートに回答してくれませんでしたから（回答を先延ばしにしたからです）」

explanation: rとaが繋がり、さらにtのように振る舞うrがフラップ化して「ﾋｱﾗ」になります。
text: No, here are our results. You actually do see that the people who wait till the last minute are so busy goofing off that they don't have any new ideas. And on the flip side, the people who race in are in such a frenzy of anxiety that they don't have original thoughts either. There's a sweet spot where originals seem to live. Why is this? Maybe original people just have bad work habits. Maybe procrastinating does not cause creativity. To find out, we designed some experiments. We asked people to generate new business ideas, and then we get independent readers to evaluate how creative and useful they are. And some of them are asked to do the task right away. Others we randomly assign to procrastinate by dangling Minesweeper in front of them for either five or 10 minutes. And sure enough, the moderate procrastinators are 16 percent more creative than the other two groups.
Japanese: No, here are our results. You actually do see that the people who wait till the last minute are so busy goofing off that they don't have any new ideas. いえ、こちらが私たちの調査結果です。実際、ギリギリまで物事を引き延ばす人々は、遊び回るのに忙しすぎて、新しいアイデアを全く思いつかないことがわかります。  And on the flip side, the people who race in are in such a frenzy of anxiety that they don't have original thoughts either. There's a sweet spot where originals seem to live. Why is this? Maybe original people just have bad work habits. Maybe procrastinating does not cause creativity. そしてその一方で、何でも急いで終わらせようとする人々は、不安でパニック状態にあるため、やはり独創的な考えを持つことができません。「オリジナルズ（独創的な人たち）」が存在する、ちょうど良いバランス（スイートスポット）があるのです。なぜこうなるのでしょうか？ おそらく、独創的な人々は単に仕事の習慣が悪いだけかもしれません。あるいは、先延ばしにすることが創造性を引き起こしているわけではないのかもしれません。  To find out, we designed some experiments. We asked people to generate new business ideas, and then we get independent readers to evaluate how creative and useful they are. And some of them are asked to do the task right away. Others we randomly assign to procrastinate by dangling Minesweeper in front of them for either five or 10 minutes. And sure enough, the moderate procrastinators are 16 percent more creative than the other two groups. それを確かめるために、私たちはいくつかの実験を設計しました。被験者に新しいビジネスのアイデアを考えてもらい、その後、第三者の評価者にその独創性と有用性を評価してもらいました。被験者のうち一部にはすぐに課題に取り組んでもらい、他のグループには、マインスイーパー（ゲーム）を5分間、あるいは10分間提示して、ランダムに「先延ばし」をするよう割り当てました。すると案の定、適度に先延ばしをしたグループは、他の2つのグループよりも創造性が16パーセント高いという結果が出たのです。

explanation: s(z音)とaが繋がり「ｽﾞｵｰｻﾑ」になります。
text: Now, Minesweeper is awesome, but it's not the driver of the effect, because if you play the game first before you learn about the task, there's no creativity boost. It's only when you're told that you're going to be working on this problem, and then you start procrastinating, but the task is still active in the back of your mind, that you start to incubate. Procrastination gives you time to consider divergent ideas, to think in nonlinear ways, to make unexpected leaps. So just as we were finishing these experiments, I was starting to write a book about originals, and I thought, "This is the perfect time to teach myself to procrastinate, while writing a chapter on procrastination." So I metaprocrastinated, and like any self-respecting precrastinator, I woke up early the next morning and I made a to-do list with steps on how to procrastinate.
Japanese: Now, Minesweeper is awesome, but it's not the driver of the effect, because if you play the game first before you learn about the task, there's no creativity boost. もちろん、マインスイーパーは素晴らしいゲームですが、それがこの効果の主因というわけではありません。なぜなら、課題について知る前にゲームをプレイしても、創造性が高まることはないからです。  It's only when you're told that you're going to be working on this problem, and then you start procrastinating, but the task is still active in the back of your mind, that you start to incubate. 「この問題に取り組んでもらいます」と告げられた後に先延ばしを始め、頭の片隅で常にその課題が意識されている状態になって初めて、アイデアの「孵化（ふか）」が始まるのです。  Procrastination gives you time to consider divergent ideas, to think in nonlinear ways, to make unexpected leaps. 先延ばしをすることで、多様なアイデアを検討し、非線形な（直線的ではない）方法で考え、予期せぬ飛躍を遂げるための時間が生まれます。  So just as we were finishing these experiments, I was starting to write a book about originals, and I thought, "This is the perfect time to teach myself to procrastinate, while writing a chapter on procrastination." ちょうどこれらの実験を終えようとしていた頃、私は『オリジナルズ』についての本を書き始めていました。そして、「先延ばしに関する章を書きながら、自分自身に先延ばしのやり方を教え込む絶好の機会だ」と考えたのです。  So I metaprocrastinated, and like any self-respecting precrastinator, I woke up early the next morning and I made a to-do list with steps on how to procrastinate. そこで私は「メタ先延ばし」をすることにしました。自尊心のある「前倒し派（プリクラスティネーター）」らしく、翌朝早くに目を覚ますと、「いかにして物事を先延ばしにするか」という手順を記したToDoリストを作成したのです。

text: And then I worked diligently toward my goal of not making progress toward my goal. explanation: kとiが繋がり「ﾄｩｯｷﾝ」になります。
text: But some of the diversions he took in optics transformed the way that he modeled light and made him into a much better painter. What about Martin Luther King, Jr.? The night before the biggest speech of his life, the March on Washington, he was up past 3am, rewriting it. He's sitting in the audience waiting for his turn to go onstage, and he is still scribbling notes and crossing out lines. When he gets onstage, 11 minutes in, he leaves his prepared remarks to utter four words that changed the course of history: "I have a dream." That was not in the script. By delaying the task of finalizing the speech until the very last minute, he left himself open to the widest range of possible ideas. And because the text wasn't set in stone, he had freedom to improvise.
Japanese: But some of the diversions he took in optics transformed the way that he modeled light and made him into a much better painter. しかし、彼が光学の分野で行ったいくつかの寄り道が、光の捉え方（モデリング）を変え、彼をより優れた画家へと成長させたのです。  What about Martin Luther King, Jr.? The night before the biggest speech of his life, the March on Washington, he was up past 3am, rewriting it. マーティン・ルーサー・キング・ジュニアはどうでしょうか？人生最大の演説である「ワシントン大行進」の前夜、彼は午前3時を過ぎてもまだ原稿を書き直していました。  He's sitting in the audience waiting for his turn to go onstage, and he is still scribbling notes and crossing out lines. When he gets onstage, 11 minutes in, he leaves his prepared remarks to utter four words that changed the course of history: "I have a dream." explanation: sとfが滑らかに繋がります。
text: Procrastinating is a vice when it comes to productivity, but it can be a virtue for creativity. What you see with a lot of great originals is that they are quick to start but they're slow to finish. And this is what I missed with Warby Parker. When they were dragging their heels for six months, I looked at them and said, "You know, a lot of other companies are starting to sell glasses online." They missed the first-mover advantage. But what I didn't realize was they were spending all that time trying to figure out how to get people to be comfortable ordering glasses online. And it turns out the first-mover advantage is mostly a myth. Look at a classic study of over 50 product categories, comparing the first movers who created the market with the improvers who introduced something different and better. explanation: kとaが繋がり「ﾙｯｶｯ」になります。
text: Look at Facebook, waiting to build a social network until after Myspace and Friendster. Look at Google, waiting for years after Altavista and Yahoo. It's much easier to improve on somebody else's idea than it is to create something new from scratch. So the lesson I learned is that to be original you don't have to be first. You just have to be different and better. But that wasn't the only reason I passed on Warby Parker. They were also full of doubts. They had backup plans lined up, and that made me doubt that they had the courage to be original. Now, on the surface, a lot of original people look confident, but behind the scenes, they feel the same fear and doubt that the rest of us do. They just manage it differently.
Japanese: Look at Facebook, waiting to build a social network until after Myspace and Friendster. Look at Google, waiting for years after Altavista and Yahoo. Facebookを見てください。彼らがソーシャルネットワークを構築したのは、MySpaceやFriendsterが登場した後のことでした。Googleを見てください。彼らはAltaVistaやYahooの登場後、何年も待っていたのです。  It's much easier to improve on somebody else's idea than it is to create something new from scratch. So the lesson I learned is that to be original you don't have to be first. You just have to be different and better. ゼロから何かを創り出すよりも、誰かのアイデアを改善する方がずっと簡単です。つまり、私が学んだ教訓は、独創的であるために「最初」である必要はないということです。ただ「他とは異なり、より優れて」いれば良いのです。  But that wasn't the only reason I passed on Warby Parker. They were also full of doubts. They had backup plans lined up, and that made me doubt that they had the courage to be original. しかし、私がワービー・パーカーへの投資を見送った理由は、それだけではありません。彼らが疑念（迷い）に満ちていたことも理由の一つです。彼らはいくつもの予備計画を用意しており、そのことが、独創的であろうとする彼らの勇気を私に疑わせたのです。  Now, on the surface, a lot of original people look confident, but behind the scenes, they feel the same fear and doubt that the rest of us do. They just manage it differently. さて、表面的には、多くの独創的な人々は自信に満ちているように見えます。しかし舞台裏では、彼らも私たちと同じように恐怖や疑念を感じています。彼らはただ、それらとの向き合い方が異なっているだけなのです。

explanation: zとoが繋がり「ｶｲﾝｽﾞｧｳﾞ」になります。
text: Now, in my research, I discovered there are two different kinds of doubt. There's self doubt and idea doubt. Self-doubt is paralyzing. It leads you to freeze. But idea doubt is energizing. It motivates you to test, to experiment, to refine, just like MLK did. And so the key to being original is just a simple thing of avoiding the leap from step three to step four. Instead of saying, "I'm crap," you say, "The first few drafts are always crap, and I'm just not there yet." So how do you get there? Well, there's a clue, it turns out, in the Internet browser that you use. We can predict your job performance and your commitment just by knowing what web browser you use. Now, some of you are not going to like the results of this study –
Japanese: Now, in my research, I discovered there are two different kinds of doubt. There's self doubt and idea doubt. さて、私の研究の中で、2種類の異なる「疑念」があることを発見しました。それは「自己疑念」と「アイデアへの疑念」です。  Self-doubt is paralyzing. It leads you to freeze. 自己疑念は、人をすくませてしまいます（麻痺させます）。それは、あなたを立ち止まらせてしまうのです。  But idea doubt is energizing. It motivates you to test, to experiment, to refine, just like MLK did. しかし、アイデアへの疑念は、活力を与えてくれます。それは、キング牧師がそうしたように、検証し、実験し、洗練させるための動機となります。  And so the key to being original is just a simple thing of avoiding the leap from step three to step four. つまり、独創的であるための鍵は、ステップ3からステップ4への飛躍を避けるという単純なことなのです。  Instead of saying, "I'm crap," you say, "The first few drafts are always crap, and I'm just not there yet." 「自分はダメだ」と言う代わりに、「最初の数案はいつもダメなものだ。私はまだそこ（完成）に到達していないだけだ」と言うのです。  So how do you get there? Well, there's a clue, it turns out, in the Internet browser that you use. では、どうすればそこに到達できるのでしょうか？ 実は、そのヒントは皆さんが使っている「インターネットブラウザ」にあることが分かりました。  We can predict your job performance and your commitment just by knowing what web browser you use. 私たちは、あなたがどのウェブブラウザを使っているかを知るだけで、あなたの仕事のパフォーマンスや貢献度を予測することができるのです。  Now, some of you are not going to like the results of this study – さて、皆さんの中には、この研究の結果を気に入らない方もいるかもしれません。

text: But there is good evidence that Firefox and Chrome users significantly outperform Internet Explorer and Safari users. Yes. (Applause) They also stay in their jobs 15 percent longer, by the way. Why? It's not a technical advantage. The four browser groups on average have similar typing speed and they also have similar levels of computer knowledge. It's about how you got the browser. Because if you use Internet Explorer or Safari, those came preinstalled on your computer, and you accepted the default option that was handed to you. If you wanted Firefox or Chrome, you had to doubt the default and ask, is there a different option out there, and then be a little resourceful and download a new browser. So people hear about this study and they're like, "Great, if I want to get better at my job, I just need to upgrade my browser?"
Japanese: But there is good evidence that Firefox and Chrome users significantly outperform Internet Explorer and Safari users. Yes. (Applause) しかし、FirefoxとChromeのユーザーは、Internet ExplorerやSafariのユーザーよりも明らかに高い成果を上げているという確かな証拠があります。その通りです。（拍手）  They also stay in their jobs 15 percent longer, by the way. ちなみに、彼らは同じ職場に留まる期間も15パーセント長いのです。  Why? It's not a technical advantage. なぜでしょうか？ それは技術的な優位性によるものではありません。  The four browser groups on average have similar typing speed and they also have similar levels of computer knowledge. これら4つのブラウザ・グループを平均すると、タイピング速度は同程度であり、コンピュータに関する知識レベルもほぼ同じなのです。  It's about how you got the browser. 重要なのは、そのブラウザを「どうやって手に入れたか」という点にあります。  Because if you use Internet Explorer or Safari, those came preinstalled on your computer, and you accepted the default option that was handed to you. なぜなら、Internet ExplorerやSafariを使っている場合、それらはコンピュータに最初からインストールされており、あなたは与えられた「初期設定（デフォルト）」という選択肢をそのまま受け入れたことになるからです。  If you wanted Firefox or Chrome, you had to doubt the default and ask, is there a different option out there, and then be a little resourceful and download a new browser. 一方で、FirefoxやChromeを使いたいと思ったら、デフォルトの状態に疑問を抱き、「他に別の選択肢はないだろうか？」と問いかけ、自ら工夫して新しいブラウザをダウンロードする必要があったのです。  So people hear about this study and they're like, "Great, if I want to get better at my job, I just need to upgrade my browser?" ですから、この研究について聞いた人々はこう言います。「なるほど、仕事ができるようになりたければ、単にブラウザをアップグレードすればいいんだね？」

text: No, it's about being the kind of person who takes the initiative to doubt the default and look for a better option. And if you do that well, you will open yourself up to the opposite of déjà vu. There's a name for it. It's called vuja de. (Laughter) Vuja de is when you look at something you've seen many times before and all of a sudden see it with fresh eyes. It's a screenwriter who looks at a movie script that can't get the green light for more than half a century. In every past version, the main character has been an evil queen. But Jennifer Lee starts to question whether that makes sense. She rewrites the first act, reinvents the villain as a tortured hero and Frozen becomes the most successful animated movie ever. So there's a simple message from this story. When you feel doubt, don't let it go.
Japanese: No, it's about being the kind of person who takes the initiative to doubt the default and look for a better option. And if you do that well, you will open yourself up to the opposite of déjà vu. There's a name for it. It's called vuja de. (Laughter) いいえ、重要なのは、初期設定（デフォルト）を疑い、より良い選択肢を探そうと自ら進んで行動するような人間になることなのです。そして、それをうまく実践できれば、「デジャヴ（既視感）」の反対の状態へと自分を導くことができます。それには名前があります。「ヴジャデ（未視感）」と呼ばれています。（笑い）  Vuja de is when you look at something you've seen many times before and all of a sudden see it with fresh eyes. It's a screenwriter who looks at a movie script that can't get the green light for more than half a century. In every past version, the main character has been an evil queen. ヴジャデとは、何度も目にしてきたものを、突然新鮮な視点で見ることを指します。例えば、半世紀以上もの間、制作の許可（グリーンライト）が下りなかった映画の脚本に目を向けたある脚本家がいました。過去のあらゆるバージョンにおいて、その主人公は「邪悪な女王」として描かれていました。  But Jennifer Lee starts to question whether that makes sense. She rewrites the first act, reinvents the villain as a tortured hero and Frozen becomes the most successful animated movie ever. So there's a simple message from this story. When you feel doubt, don't let it go. しかし、ジェニファー・リーはそれが本当に理にかなっているのかと疑問を抱き始めます。彼女は第1幕を書き直し、悪役を「苦悩する主人公」として作り変えました。その結果、『アナと雪の女王（Frozen）』は史上最も成功したアニメーション映画となったのです。この物語が伝えるメッセージはシンプルです。疑念を感じた時、それをそのまま流してはいけません。

text: What about fear? Originals feel fear, too. They're afraid of failing, but what sets them apart from the rest of us is that they're even more afraid of failing to try. They know you can fail by starting a business that goes bankrupt or by failing to start a business at all. They know that in the long run, our biggest regrets are not our actions but our inactions. The things we wish we could redo, if you look at the science, are the chances not taken. Elon Musk told me recently, he didn't expect Tesla to succeed. He was sure the first few SpaceX launches would fail to make it to orbit, let alone get back, but it was too important not to try. And for so many of us, when we have an important idea, we don't bother to try. But I have some good news for you. You are not going to get judged on your bad ideas. A lot of people think they will. If you look across industries and ask people about their biggest idea, their most important suggestion, 85 percent of them stayed silent instead of speaking up.
Japanese: What about fear? Originals feel fear, too. They're afraid of failing, but what sets them apart from the rest of us is that they're even more afraid of failing to try. 恐怖についてはどうでしょうか？独創的な人々もまた、恐怖を感じます。彼らも失敗を恐れますが、私たちと彼らを分かつのは、彼らが「挑戦しないこと」をさらに恐れているという点です。  They know you can fail by starting a business that goes bankrupt or by failing to start a business at all. They know that in the long run, our biggest regrets are not our actions but our inactions. 彼らは、起業して倒産することで失敗することもあれば、そもそも起業しないことでも失敗し得るということを知っています。長期的には、私たちの最大の不平（後悔）は「したこと」ではなく「しなかったこと」にあると、彼らは理解しているのです。  The things we wish we could redo, if you look at the science, are the chances not taken. 科学的な知見に基づけば、私たちが「やり直したい」と願うのは、掴み損ねたチャンスなのです。  Elon Musk told me recently, he didn't expect Tesla to succeed. He was sure the first few SpaceX launches would fail to make it to orbit, let alone get back, but it was too important not to try. イーロン・マスクは最近私に、テスラが成功するとは思っていなかったと語りました。彼は、SpaceXの最初の数回の打ち上げは、帰還どころか軌道に乗ることさえ失敗するだろうと確信していましたが、挑戦しないわけにはいかないほどそれは重要なことだったのです。  And for so many of us, when we have an important idea, we don't bother to try. しかし私たちの多くは、重要なアイデアを思いついても、あえて挑戦しようとはしません。  But I have some good news for you. You are not going to get judged on your bad ideas. A lot of people think they will. ですが、良いニュースがあります。あなたは、自分の「ダメなアイデア」によって評価されることはありません。多くの人が、そう（評価されると）思い込んでしまっていますが。  If you look across industries and ask people about their biggest idea, their most important suggestion, 85 percent of them stayed silent instead of speaking up. あらゆる業界を調査し、最大級のアイデアや最も重要な提案について尋ねてみたところ、85パーセントの人々が、声を上げる代わりに沈黙を守っていました。

They know that in the long run, our biggest regrets are not our actions but our inactions. 彼らは、起業して倒産することで失敗することもあれば、そもそも起業しないことでも失敗し得るということを知っています。長期的には、私たちの最大の不平（後悔）は「したこと」ではなく「しなかったこと」にあると、彼らは理解しているのです。  The things we wish we could redo, if you look at the science, are the chances not taken. 科学的な知見に基づけば、私たちが「やり直したい」と願うのは、掴み損ねたチャンスなのです。  Elon Musk told me recently, he didn't expect Tesla to succeed. He was sure the first few SpaceX launches would fail to make it to orbit, let alone get back, but it was too important not to try. イーロン・マスクは最近私に、テスラが成功するとは思っていなかったと語りました。彼は、SpaceXの最初の数回の打ち上げは、帰還どころか軌道に乗ることさえ失敗するだろうと確信していましたが、挑戦しないわけにはいかないほどそれは重要なことだったのです。  And for so many of us, when we have an important idea, we don't bother to try. しかし私たちの多くは、重要なアイデアを思いついても、あえて挑戦しようとはしません。  But I have some good news for you. You are not going to get judged on your bad ideas. A lot of people think they will. ですが、良いニュースがあります。あなたは、自分の「ダメなアイデア」によって評価されることはありません。多くの人が、そう（評価されると）思い込んでしまっていますが。  If you look across industries and ask people about their biggest idea, their most important suggestion, 85 percent of them stayed silent instead of speaking up. あらゆる業界を調査し、最大級のアイデアや最も重要な提案について尋ねてみたところ、85パーセントの人々が、声を上げる代わりに沈黙を守っていました。

text: What about fear? Originals feel fear, too. They're afraid of failing, but what sets them apart from the rest of us is that they're even more afraid of failing to try. They know you can fail by starting a business that goes bankrupt or by failing to start a business at all. They know that in the long run, our biggest regrets are not our actions but our inactions. The things we wish we could redo, if you look at the science, are the chances not taken. Elon Musk told me recently, he didn't expect Tesla to succeed. He was sure the first few SpaceX launches would fail to make it to orbit, let alone get back, but it was too important not to try. And for so many of us, when we have an important idea, we don't bother to try. But I have some good news for you. You are not going to get judged on your bad ideas. A lot of people think they will. If you look across industries and ask people about their biggest idea, their most important suggestion, 85 percent of them stayed silent instead of speaking up.

explanation: dとoが繋がり「ｱﾌﾚｲﾀﾞｳﾞ」になります。
text: They were afraid of embarrassing themselves, of looking stupid. But guess what? Originals have lots and lots of bad ideas, tons of them, in fact. If you look across fields, the greatest originals are the ones who fail the most, because they're the ones who try the most. Take classical composers, the best of the best. Why do some of them get more pages in encyclopedias than others and also have their compositions rerecorded more times? One of the best predictors is the sheer volume of compositions that they generate. The more output you churn out, the more variety you get and the better your chances of stumbling on something truly original. Even the three icons of classical music -- Bach, Beethoven, Mozart -- had to generate hundreds and hundreds of compositions to come up with a much smaller number of masterpieces.
Japanese: They were afraid of embarrassing themselves, of looking stupid. But guess what? Originals have lots and lots of bad ideas, tons of them, in fact. 彼らは恥をかくことや、馬鹿げていると思われることを恐れていました。ですが、実はどうでしょう？ 独創的な人々は、数多くのダメなアイデアを出しています。実際、山ほど出しているのです。  If you look across fields, the greatest originals are the ones who fail the most, because they're the ones who try the most. あらゆる分野を見渡してみると、最も偉大な独創者とは、最も多く失敗した人たちです。なぜなら、彼らこそが最も多く挑戦した人たちだからです。  Take classical composers, the best of the best. クラシック音楽の作曲家、それも最高峰の人たちを例に挙げてみましょう。  Why do some of them get more pages in encyclopedias than others and also have their compositions rerecorded more times? なぜ、ある作曲家は他の人よりも百科事典のページ数が多く、その作品が何度も繰り返し録音されるのでしょうか？  One of the best predictors is the sheer volume of compositions that they generate. 最も有力な予測因子の一つは、彼らが生み出した作品の「純粋な量」です。  The more output you churn out, the more variety you get and the better your chances of stumbling on something truly original. アウトプットを量産すればするほど、多様性が生まれ、真に独創的な何かに巡り合うチャンスが高まるのです。  Even the three icons of classical music -- Bach, Beethoven, Mozart -- had to generate hundreds and hundreds of compositions to come up with a much smaller number of masterpieces. クラシック音楽の3大巨匠であるバッハ、ベートーヴェン、モーツァルトでさえ、ごくわずかな傑作を生み出すために、何百もの作品を作らなければなりませんでした。

explanation: withoutのtがﾌﾗｯﾌﾟ化し､doing aの連結と重なり滑らかになります。
text: Now, you may be wondering, how did this guy become great without doing a whole lot? I don't know how Wagner pulled that off. But for most of us, if we want to be more original, we have to generate more ideas. The Warby Parker founders, when they were trying to name their company, they needed something sophisticated, unique, with no negative associations to build a retail brand, and they tested over 2,000 possibilities before they finally put together Warby and Parker. So if you put all this together, what you see is that originals are not that different from the rest of us. They feel fear and doubt. They procrastinate. They have bad ideas. And sometimes, it's not in spite of those qualities but because of them that they succeed.
Japanese: Now, you may be wondering, how did this guy become great without doing a whole lot? I don't know how Wagner pulled that off. But for most of us, if we want to be more original, we have to generate more ideas. さて、皆さんは「この男（ワーグナー）は大したこともせずに、どうやって偉大になれたのだろう」と不思議に思っているかもしれません。ワーグナーがどうやってそれをやってのけたのか、私には分かりません。しかし、私たちのほとんどにとって、より独創的でありたいと願うなら、より多くのアイデアを生み出さなければならないのです。  The Warby Parker founders, when they were trying to name their company, they needed something sophisticated, unique, with no negative associations to build a retail brand, and they tested over 2,000 possibilities before they finally put together Warby and Parker. ワービー・パーカーの創業者たちは、社名を決めようとしていた際、小売ブランドを構築するために、洗練されていてユニークで、かつネガティブな連想を抱かせない名前を必要としていました。そして彼らは、最終的に「ワービー」と「パーカー」を組み合わせるまでに、2,000以上の候補を検証したのです。  So if you put all this together, what you see is that originals are not that different from the rest of us. They feel fear and doubt. They procrastinate.
